elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we address the challenging task of multimodal mathematical reasoning by incorporating the ability of ``slow thinking" into multimodal large language models (MLLMs).
elements.sent-2411-002: Contrary to existing methods that rely on direct or fast thinking, our key idea is to construct long chains of thought (CoT) consisting of atomic actions in a step-by-step manner, guiding MLLMs to perform complex reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-003: To this end, we design a novel AtomThink framework composed of three key modules: (i) a CoT annotation engine that automatically generates high-quality CoT annotations to address the lack of high-quality visual mathematical data; (ii) an atomic step fine-tuning strategy that jointly optimizes an MLLM and a policy reward model (PRM) for step-wise reasoning; and (iii) four different search strategies that can be applied with the PRM to complete reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-004: Additionally, we propose AtomMATH, a large-scale multimodal dataset of long CoTs, and an atomic capability evaluation metric for mathematical tasks.
elements.sent-2411-005: Extensive experimental results show that the proposed AtomThink significantly improves the performance of baseline MLLMs, achieving approximately 50\% relative accuracy gains on MathVista and 120\% on MathVerse.
elements.sent-2411-006: To support the advancement of multimodal slow-thinking models, we will make our code and dataset publicly available on https://github.com/Quinn777/AtomThink.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{img/radar.pdf} \caption{Atomic capability evaluation of different models. Existing open-source models exhibit significant shortcomings in capabilities such as variable definition, approximation and image description.} \label{fig:ability_distribution}
elements.sent-2411-007: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning~\cite{wei2022chain has provided a novel scheme for large language models (LLMs) to tackle complex reasoning tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-008: By utilizing a small number of specially designed instructions, CoT enables LLMs to generate intermediate reasoning steps, significantly enhancing performance on symbolic tasks such as mathematical problems and code writing~\cite{zhao2023survey .}
elements.sent-2411-009: While CoT-based methods show clear improvements over direct predictions, they still rely heavily on greedy decoding strategies.
elements.sent-2411-010: More recently, the introduction of OpenAI's o1~\cite{openai2024_o1 marks a substantial advancement in the ability of artificial intelligence systems to perform high-level reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Unlike traditional models, o1 excels in solving complex problems by utilizing extended reasoning chains and adopting test-time scaling, i.e., ``slow thinking".
elements.sent-2411-012: In addition to o1, several concurrent works have explored methods for incorporating slow thinking capabilities into open-source LLMs, such as Thought Trees~\cite{yao2024tree and Monte Carlo tree search (MCTS) based tree search techniques~\cite{gao2024interpretable,qin2024o1,wang2024q,wang2024openr .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The success of o1 and its variants demonstrate that incorporating slow thinking into LLMs significantly enhances their performance on complex, multi-step tasks, improving their overall problem-solving capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, adopting the slow-thinking technique into multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is challenging, due to the increased data and computational resource demand for information modeling in visual tasks ~\cite{zhang2024improve,qi2024cogcom .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Although many efforts have been conducted to alleviate this issue, such as incorporating interleaved image-text data ~\cite{alayrac2022flamingo , prompt engineering ~\cite{shao2024visual,ni2024visual , they are still confined to stimulating the inherent CoT capabilities of MLLMs, without considering the quality of each intermediate step in the reasoning chain.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Hence, existing methods are hard to apply test-time scaling laws to guarantee their performance.
elements.sent-2411-017: To validate the importance of the quality of each intermediate step in CoT, we first design a capability evaluation method to perform a fine-grained quality assessment of each atomic step generated by MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-018: Here we define an atomic step as the minimal prediction node in the slow thinking process.
elements.sent-2411-019: Considering that humans may utilize distinct cognitive abilities for solving mathematical problems, we utilize one of the current most advanced LLMs, i.e., GPT-4o~\cite{openai2024_4o to construct an ability set and estimate scores of atomic steps with outcome supervision.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The results shown in Figure \ref{fig:ability_distribution indicate that the step quality of existing open-source models is significantly lower than that of GPT-4o, particularly in areas such as image recognition, variable definition, and calculation ability.}
elements.sent-2411-021: This finding further motivates our focus on the capability gaps in existing models, prompting us to improve performance by enhancing the quality of atomic reasoning steps.
elements.sent-2411-022: Therefore, to fully leverage the advantages of CoT and address the aforementioned challenges, we propose a full-process slow-thinking framework called AtomThink.
elements.sent-2411-023: AtomThink introduces a multimodal CoT annotation engine, an atomic step finetuning strategy, and policy searching strategies to generate high-quality atomic steps.
elements.sent-2411-024: It aims to enhance the decoding capabilities of MLLMs through careful training, combined with post-sampling search strategies to identify the optimal prediction nodes.
elements.sent-2411-025: To begin with, the proposed annotation engine is used to create a novel multimodal long CoT dataset including 26k high-level mathematical problems, 157k atomic-granularity steps, and 130k process supervision annotations.
elements.sent-2411-026: The construction of this dataset does not require manual labeling and effectively leverages existing short labels.
elements.sent-2411-027: Secondly, our atomic step finetuning strategy applies step-level masking to the training set, forcing our models to learn multi-turn self-dialogue ability and generate reasoning focused on individual inference actions.
elements.sent-2411-028: Thirdly, we explore different search strategies along both the path and step dimensions during the inference phase to find optimal prediction nodes.
elements.sent-2411-029: To validate the effectiveness of our method, we conduct extensive experiments on public datasets.
elements.sent-2411-030: We improved the accuracy of LLaVA-Llama3-8B on MathVista and MathVerse by 9.6\% and 18.8\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-031: With EMOVA (8B) as the base model, AtomThink achieved the highest accuracy of 40.5\% on MathVerse, surpassing the cutting-edge GPT-4V.
elements.sent-2411-032: In summary, our primary contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-033: We introduce AtomThink, a comprehensive framework that guides MLLMs to focus on atomic step reasoning, which obtains consistent performance improvements across multiple baseline MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-034: By designing an atomic capability evaluation based on outcome supervision, we reveal the capability distribution of MLLMs in generating each type of atomic step.
elements.sent-2411-035: A multimodal long CoT dataset specifically focused on multimodal mathematical tasks, AtomMATH, is first introduced.
elements.paragraph-2411-001: Slow Thinking in Multimodal Reasoning Tasks
elements.sent-2411-036: Complex reasoning tasks such as mathematical computation and code programming have long been challenging for MLLMs~\cite{yin2023survey,zhao2023survey,liu2023mathematical .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Some prior work has approached this issue from the perspective of prompt engineering, encouraging models to generate Chain-of-Thought(CoT), which is widely believed to enhance model's reasoning \cite{wei2022chain,wang2022self .}
elements.sent-2411-038: They carefully modify the input distribution to enable the model to mimic human step-by-step output without finetuning parameters.
elements.sent-2411-039: Other recent studies have explored understanding visual ambiguity by introducing multi-turn chain-of-thoughts \cite{ni2024visual .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Shao et al. \cite{shao2024visual have considered incorporating additional visual tokens into CoTs, such as object regions and precise localization.}
elements.sent-2411-041: However, due to the lack of multimodal process supervision data, current works have not explored reward model-based search strategies, which are widely used in LLMs \cite{uesato2022solving,zelikman2022star,lightman2023let,brown2024large,snell2024scaling .}
elements.paragraph-2411-002: Long CoT Annotation for Mathematical Data
elements.sent-2411-042: The introduction of slow thinking relies heavily on the availability of high-quality step-level annotations.
elements.sent-2411-043: In 2023, Lightman et al. \cite{lightmanlet constructed a process supervision dataset composed of extensive human annotations, which has been widely used for mathematical reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Recent advancements have focused on automating the data acquisition process, allowing models to generate their own CoT.
elements.sent-2411-045: Techniques like Quiet-STaR \cite{zelikman2024quiet have demonstrated how self-generated reasoning can enhance model performance without requiring manually labels.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Moreover, some methods based on Monte Carlo estimation have automated the process data collection, but they also introduce additional computational cost \cite{Math-shepherd,OmegaPRM .}
elements.sent-2411-047: In multimodal domain, MAVIS \cite{zhang2024mavis , a dataset consisting of 834k visual math problems annotated with short CoT, has been proposed.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Other studies have distilled reasoning processes from short answers \cite{zhang2024improve .}
elements.sent-2411-049: However, these machine-generated annotations are often too brief and challenging to segment semantically.

During inference, step-wise or path-wise searching strategies can be applied to find optimal policies.} \label{fig:framework}
elements.sent-2411-050: In this section, we present the details of AtomThink for promoting MLLMs for mathematical reasoning with slow thinking.
elements.sent-2411-051: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:framework , AtomThink consists of three key components, including a multimodal CoT annotation engine (Sec.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The annotation engine is designed to efficiently generate long CoTs to address data scarcity.
elements.sent-2411-053: With sufficient data, we fine-tune MLLMs and train a process reward model (PRM) for incorporating slow thinking ability into models.
elements.sent-2411-054: Furthermore, we explore four different path-wise and step-wise strategies for policy searching, allowing the fine-tuned MLLM to ensure that each decision made during its inference contributes to the overall accuracy and consistency of reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-055: Finally, we propose an atomic capability evaluation metric in Sec.

elements.sent-2411-056: Guiding MLLMs toward deep reasoning requires a substantial amount of high-quality CoT data.
elements.sent-2411-057: However, in the field of visual mathematics, the scarcity of publicly available datasets presents a considerable challenge.
elements.sent-2411-058: To overcome this, we develop an automated data engine capable of generating step-by-step long CoTs, resulting in our own atomic mathematical problem dataset, dubbed AtomMATH.
elements.sent-2411-059: Specifically, our data engine introduces a dynamic prompting strategy and a semantic-level augmentation strategy to produce multi-step reasoning paths.
elements.sent-2411-060: To overcome the computational cost limitations associated with previous methods that relied on manual annotation or process supervision, we explore the possibility of driving existing models to autonomously generate high-quality reasoning data through simple prompting.
elements.sent-2411-061: Inspired by recent research \cite{g1 on using prompting strategies to improve the reasoning capabilities of LLMs, we propose a dynamic prompt strategy for generating atomic inference steps.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Specifically, our strategy drives LLMs to iteratively construct state-reasoning paths.
elements.sent-2411-063: Each path node represents a reasoning step and encompasses the previous stage, the current state, and a possible action.
elements.sent-2411-064: The possible action includes continuing reasoning, verifying and drawing conclusion, which is determined by LLM itself.
elements.sent-2411-065: Unlike previous methods such as OmegaPRM \cite{OmegaPRM and Math-Shepherd \cite{Math-shepherd that generate a whole reasoning tree at once, our approach implicitly integrates the search over the step dimension into existing reasoning process through prompt engineering.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: For each problem instance, only a single valid path is explored, eliminating the need for additional process supervision computation.
elements.sent-2411-067: To fully leverage existing short CoT annotations of VQA datasets, we also employ LLMs to atomize and augment these annotations.
elements.sent-2411-068: An example of short CoT augmentation is provided in the supplemental material.
elements.sent-2411-069: This approach allows us to semantically segment an original reasoning process into multiple discrete steps, and focus on solving a single atomic problem at each stage of the reasoning process, thereby ensuring the clarity and precision of our model.
elements.sent-2411-070: We sample mathematical data from Geo3k \cite{geo3k , Mathv360k \cite{shi2024math , MMMU-dev \cite{yue2024mmmu , TabMWP \cite{TabMWP , CLEVR \cite{johnson2017clevr , Geomverse-Cauldron \cite{kazemi2024geomverse and MAVIS \cite{zhang2024mavis .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: For Geomverse and MAVIS, we conduct short CoT augmentation, while the rest are generated by dynamic prompts to produce multi-step reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-072: Both short CoT augmentation and dynamic prompting are implemented by GPT-4o in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-073: After generating long CoTs, we also use GPT-4o to double-check the answers and remove rollouts with incorrect responses.
elements.sent-2411-074: To enable our model to learn atomic step-based reasoning patterns, we progressively mask each node along its reasoning path to generate 157k atomic steps.
elements.sent-2411-075: We refer to this database as AMATH-SFT.
elements.sent-2411-076: Meanwhile, we sampled approximately 65k examples with correct steps from AMATH-SFT, and generated negative samples using GPT-4o to serve as PRM training data.
elements.sent-2411-077: Table \ref{tab:dataset illustrates the distribution of our data.}
elements.sent-2411-078: In Table \ref{tab:diff_data_style , we also evaluate the quality in a subset of 500 AtomMATH samples with GPT-4o.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Result shows that our method exhibits longer information content than general CoT.
elements.sent-2411-080: Even compared to the PRM800k with golden annotations, our data obtained a higher preference score.
elements.sent-2411-081: To fully exploit MLLMs for addressing multi-modal mathematical problems, we conduct fine-tuning with atomic step-wise reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-082: Particularly, this process includes fine-tuning the MLLM on our AtomMATH dataset and learning the PRM to estimate reward scores during the inference.
elements.sent-2411-083: To transfer MLLM to step-wise mathematical reasoning, we first fine-tune it within the framework of Markov decision process (MDP) learning.
elements.sent-2411-084: Specifically, we consider the reasoning process of MLLM as an MDP, which can be formulated as $M=(V, S, A, R, \pi)$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Here, \(V\) denotes the vocabulary, \(S\) represents historical reasoning steps, and \(A\) corresponds to next atomic step predicted by MLLM.
elements.sent-2411-086: Hereby we can adopt the visual instruction tuning technique \cite{liu2024visual to fine-tune MLLM.}
elements.sent-2411-087: In a slow thinking process, reasoning is carried out step by step, where each atomic step provides an intermediate conclusion.
elements.sent-2411-088: We train the PRM to implement \( \pi(a|s) \) and provide feedback for every step, allowing MLLMs to refine and improve its reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-089: Formally, given the description of mathematical problem $q$, for an arbitrary step \( t \ge 1\), the PRM predicts a probability \( p_t \) of selecting an action \( a\) given the previous states \( s_{1:t-1 \) as follows: \begin{equation}p_t(a) = PRM\left( [q, s_{1:t-1}], a\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-090: We propose to train the PRM by minimizing the following binary cross-entropy loss: \begin{equation}L_{PRM} = \sum^T_{t=1}{y_{t}(a)\log p_t(a)} + (1-y_{t}(a))\log(1-p_t(a)), \label{eq:lprm}\end{equation} where $y_t(a)$ denotes the ground-truth CoT annotation that $y_t = 1$ if the action $a$ is selected, otherwise $y_t(a) = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-091: Note that we omit to enumerate all possible actions in Eq. (\ref{eq:lprm ) for the concise presentation.}
elements.sent-2411-092: After selecting the action at the current step $a_t$, we concatenate it with the previous states to construct $s_t$, i.e., $s_t = s_{1:t-1 \cup a_t$.}
elements.sent-2411-093: In this subsection, we perform atomic step fine-tuning on the AtomMATH (including A-PRM and A-SFT subsets) and PRM800k dataset \cite{lightmanlet .}
elements.sent-2411-094: Moreover, we incorporate image captions into the generation of long CoT data, thus we can alleviate the expensive computation burden of image understanding in MLLMs and focus on texts for supervised fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-095: Therefore, we post-train an LLM based on Math-psa \cite{wang2024openr to evaluate the consistency of atomic texts and supervise fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-096: With the fine-tuned MLLM capable of atomic step reasoning and the well-trained PRM providing feedback, we can now begin the reasoning process.
elements.sent-2411-097: As there are many search strategies to generate candidate actions, we categorize the existing strategies into path-wise searching and step-wise searching and explore them in our AtomThink framework.
elements.sent-2411-098: In path-wise searching, we build upon prior work \cite{wang2024openr,snell2024scaling by parallel sampling multiple paths and aggregating scores to find optimal solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-099: We investigate the following two strategies:.
elements.sent-2411-100: Majority Voting: It combines multiple reasoning paths by selecting the most frequent outcome across them.
elements.sent-2411-101: It assumes that the consensus across different paths is more likely to lead to the correct answer.
elements.sent-2411-102: Best-of-N: Given a generative MLLM, the best-of-N sampling method generates \(C\) candidate rollouts simultaneously and selects the solution with the highest score.
elements.sent-2411-103: The evaluation of candidate reasoning processes is determined by the PRM, which employs three aggregation methods to map the dense scores to the overall value of the entire path: 1) The worst action: Compare the worst action among all candidate rollouts.
elements.sent-2411-104: It penalizes solutions with any weak action and is used to search a reasoning that is sensitive to errors.
elements.sent-2411-105: The explainability and consistency of intermediate reasoning are emphasized here as important as the outcome.
elements.sent-2411-106: Searching strategies of this type start with an initial path and incrementally expand the sampling space for each atomic action.
elements.sent-2411-107: Beam search and greedy strategies are applied to prune branches with low quality.
elements.sent-2411-108: Greedy Algorithm: It focuses on making the locally optimal choice at each step of the reasoning process.
elements.sent-2411-109: It selects the best immediate action (step) based on the current state, without considering future consequences.
elements.sent-2411-110: Beam Search: It explores multiple branches at each action and maintains a fixed number of top candidates for each stage of reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-111: It balances between exploring different paths and exploiting the most promising ones.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{img/eval.pdf} \caption{Atomic capability evaluation. The capability categories are derived from the clustering of GPT-4o's behavior. By sampling each atomic step and evaluating the accessibility of the results, we assign a soft label that represents quality of an atomic step.} \label{fig:eval}
elements.sent-2411-112: Similar to human problem-solving processes, a CoT may involve the use of multiple reasoning abilities.
elements.sent-2411-113: However, traditional CoT methods do not focus on the quality of individual reasoning steps or provide fine-grained analyses of the underlying abilities.
elements.sent-2411-114: To address this gap, we have developed an atomic capability evaluation strategy, offering a new analytical perspective for slow thinking.
elements.sent-2411-115: Our evaluation method aims to assess the mathematical capabilities of a target model from various perspectives, such as understanding, operations, and certifications.
elements.sent-2411-116: To this end, we first need to construct a canonical set of capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-117: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:eval , we collect the behavior distribution of GPT-4o on MathVerse \cite{zhang2025mathverse and perform clustering, yielding clusters that each of them represents certain abilities utilized by high-level intelligent models in solving mathematical problems.}}
elements.sent-2411-118: Afterward, we evaluate an atomic action $a$ predicted by the target model based on outcome supervision, namely, we consider that the quality of an action can be reflected by the probability of it leading to a correct answer.
elements.sent-2411-119: This can be formulated as the following soft estimation with $K$ rounds of outcome supervision \cite{lightmanlet : \begin{equation}c_{soft}(a) = \frac{\sum^K_{k=1}\llbracket\hat{a}_{k}  is correct\rrbracket}{K},\end{equation} where $\hat{a _{k}$ denotes the final answer predicted in the $k$-th round and $\llbracket \cdot \rrbracket$ is the Iverson bracket.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Consequently, we can evaluate all the actions of the target model and map them back to the capability set, to evaluate the mathematical capabilities of the target model thoroughly.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{img/case_study.pdf} \caption{A case study of AtomThink CoT and GPT-4o generated CoT. Red and green characters denote incorrect and correct responses, respectively. Our model exhibits fewer hallucinations and stronger reasoning abilities as it focuses on the quality of atomic steps. Moreover, our model automatically decomposes the reasoning process semantically, leading to improved readability.} \label{fig:case}
elements.sent-2411-121: Our experiments utilize two open-source MLLMs, including LLaVA-Llama3-8B~\cite{liu2024visual and EMOVA-8B~\cite{chen2024emova .}}
elements.sent-2411-122: We fine-tune only the parameters of their language models and projectors with learning rates of 2e-5 and 2e-6, respectively, and a batch size of 128.
elements.sent-2411-123: We select nine cutting-edge MLLMs for comparison, including OpenAI’s o1~\cite{openai2024_o1 , 4o~\cite{openai2024_4o , and 4v~\cite{openai2024_4v , as well as LLava-NeXT-34B~\cite{liu2024llavanext , InternLM-XComposer2~\cite{zhang2024mavis , Qwen-VL-Plus~\cite{bai2023qwen , LLaVA-7B~\cite{liu2024visual , G-LLaVA-7B~\cite{gao2023gllava , and MAVIS-7B~\cite{zhang2024mavis .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: For LLaVA-Llama3-8B, we use LLaVA-665k~\cite{liu2024visual for supervised fine-tuning (SFT) as a baseline.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Formatted, we transfer the source data of AtomMATH into an aligned CoT format for incremental training, ensuring a fair comparison without introducing bells and whistles.
elements.sent-2411-128: For EMOVA-8B, we downsampled its publicly available SFT data~\cite{chen2024emova to obtain a basic post-training dataset containing about 200k samples.}
elements.sent-2411-129: For models with AtomThink, the AMATH-SFT dataset introduced in Section \ref{sec:engine , is incorporated to introduce atomic reasoning capabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-130: We evaluated the performance of our method on MathVista~\cite{lu2023mathvista , a publicly available benchmark encompassing both general-targeted and mathematics-targeted domains.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Additionally, to assess the model's ability to interpret mathematical graphs, we use a more challenging multimodal benchmark, MathVerse~\cite{zhang2025mathverse for further evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-132: It contains five categories including Text Lite (TL), Text Dominant (TD), Vision Intensive (VI), Vision Dominant (VD), Vision Only (VO).
elements.sent-2411-133: Out evaluations include four inference settings, including Direct, CoT, Quick Think, and Slow Think.
elements.sent-2411-134: In the Direct setting, we prompt the model to generate a concise final answer.
elements.sent-2411-135: In CoT, the model is instructed to answer the question through step-by-step reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-136: For the Direct and CoT evaluations, we use prompts from {lmms-eval \cite{zhang2024lmmsevalrealitycheckevaluation,lmms_eval2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-137: Our AtomThink-models support two additional settings: Quick Think and Slow Think.
elements.sent-2411-138: In Quick Think, our models follow a single, atomic reasoning path based purely on their learned policies, without employing any supplementary search strategies.
elements.sent-2411-139: In Slow Think, enhanced by the PRM, we utilize beam search with beam width of 2 and temperature of 1.0, encouraging our models to engage in more extensive reasoning.

elements.sent-2411-274: We release a high-quality, human-free annotated long-CoT dataset, AtomMATH, consisting of 157k atomic reasoning steps and 159k corresponding annotations.
elements.sent-2411-275: In this paper, we address the challenging task of multimodal mathematical reasoning by incorporating the notion of ``slow thinking'' into multimodal large language models (MLLMs).
elements.sent-2411-276: Our core idea is that models can learn to adaptively use different levels of reasoning to tackle questions of different complexity.
elements.sent-2411-277: We propose a novel paradigm of Self-structured Chain of Thought (SCoT), which comprises of minimal semantic atomic steps.
elements.sent-2411-278: Different from existing methods that rely on structured templates or free-form paradigms, our method can not only generate cognitive CoT structures for various complex tasks but also mitigates the phenomena of overthinking for easier tasks.
elements.sent-2411-279: To introduce structured reasoning into visual cognition, we further design a novel AtomThink framework with four key modules, including (i) a data engine to generate high-quality multimodal reasoning paths; (ii) a supervised fine-tuning (SFT) process with serialized inference data; (iii) a policy-guided multi-turn inference method; and (iv) an atomic capability metric to evaluate the single step utilization rate.
elements.sent-2411-280: We conduct extensive experiments to show that the proposed AtomThink significantly improves the performance of baseline MLLMs, achieving more than 10\% average accuracy gains on MathVista and MathVerse.
elements.sent-2411-281: Compared to state-of-the-art structured CoT approaches, our method not only achieves higher accuracy but also improves data utilization by 5 times and boosts inference efficiency by 85.3\%.
elements.sent-2411-282: Our code is now public available in https://github.com/Quinn777/AtomThink.

Structured-CoT follows a static reasoning template that enforces image caption even for text-dominant problems, whereas our method can adaptively chose to skip image recognition and extract information directly from the query (dynamic structures). On the other hand, unstructured CoT suffers from information redundancy where our method improves token efficiency on simple tasks (dynamic lengths).} \label{fig:intro}
elements.sent-2411-283: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning~\cite{wei2022chain constitutes a pivotal paradigm that substantially enhances the capacity of Large Language Models (LLMs) to address complex scientific problems.}
elements.sent-2411-284: This methodology facilitates emergent intermediate reasoning steps within LLMs, exemplified by significant advances in frontier Large Reasoning Models (LRMs)~\cite{openai2024_o1,guo2025deepseek .}
elements.sent-2411-285: These models solve intricate problems through extended reasoning chains and test-time scaling, often conceptualized as "slow thinking"~\cite{slowthinking .}
elements.sent-2411-286: Recent research has focused on elucidating internal reasoning mechanisms in frontier LRMs~\cite{yao2024tree,gao2024interpretable,qin2024o1,wang2024openr .}
elements.sent-2411-287: Approaches such as LLaVA-CoT~\cite{llavacot and LlamaV-o1~\cite{llamavo1 implement Structured CoT through fixed modules driven by manually defined templates, constraining reasoning diversity in multimodal contexts.}}
elements.sent-2411-288: In contrast, models including OpenAI-o1~\cite{openai2024_o1 and DeepSeek-R1~\cite{guo2025deepseek employ Unstructured CoT, which eliminates predefined frameworks to autonomously generate emergent free-form reasoning chains via iterative refinement.}}
elements.sent-2411-289: Although Unstructured CoT better approximates human cognition and demonstrates superior generalization, recent investigations~\cite{chen2024not,wang2025thoughts reveal these slow-thinking models suffer from inefficient token utilization and overthinking tendencies when processing simpler problems.}
elements.sent-2411-290: As Figure~\ref{fig:intro illustrates, both paradigms exhibit significant limitations.}
elements.sent-2411-291: Consequently, we establish two fundamental principles: different problems demand distinct reasoning capabilities, and reasoning chain complexity should align with problem difficulty for optimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-292: To dynamically generate appropriate reasoning structures for problems with diverse complexity, we introduce a novel paradigm of Self-structured Chain-of-Thought (SCoT), which decomposes reasoning process into atomic steps.
elements.sent-2411-293: To activate the model's self-structured reasoning abilities in multimodal tasks, we further develop a full-process slow-thinking framework called AtomThink.
elements.sent-2411-294: It comprises four key components, including a data engine, supervised fine-tuning, policy search and atomic capability evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-295: To begin with, a data annotation engine with novel prompting and bad-case filtering strategies is used to create a novel multimodal long CoT dataset.

elements.sent-2411-297: Furthermore, our atomic step finetuning strategy applies step-level masking to the training set, forcing our models to learn individual inference steps.
elements.sent-2411-298: During the inference phase, the model is not only capable of spontaneously generating CoT in fast-thinking mode, but also continuously improve with process supervision models and step search mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-299: Lastly, we propose an atomic capability evaluation metric based on reasoning behavior clustering and step utilization calculation, which quantitatively shows the model's capability in utilizing individual atomic steps.

elements.sent-2411-302: Our approach achieves 500\% higher data utilization than previous frontier approach LLaVA-CoT while maintaining superior performance, and we offer enhanced inference efficiency by over 80\%.

elements.sent-2411-304: Our primary contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-305: We introduce Self-structured Chain-of-Thought as a new thinking paradigm to decompose any reasoning process into atomic steps.
elements.sent-2411-306: It eliminates the need for constructing structured thought templates and achieves significant improvements in both data utilization and inference efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-307: We offer a comprehensive AtomThink framework including plug-and-play modules for data annotation, atomic fine-tuning, multi-turn inference and capability evaluation, is designed to improve the reasoning ability of MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-308: We validate the effectiveness of our approach on three widely-adopted multimodal reasoning benchmarks across various MLLMs of different sizes.
elements.sent-2411-309: Additionally, we present a fine-grained analysis of comprehension capabilities distribution.
elements.subsection-2411-018: Chain of Thought in Multimodal Reasoning Tasks
elements.sent-2411-310: Complex reasoning tasks such as mathematical computation have long been challenging for MLLMs~\cite{yin2023survey,liu2023mathematical .}
elements.sent-2411-311: Some prior work has approached this issue from the perspective of prompt engineering, encouraging models to generate CoT, which is widely believed to enhance reasoning abilities \cite{wei2022chain,wang2022self .}
elements.sent-2411-312: They carefully modify the input distribution to generate unstructured reasoning path without finetuning parameters.
elements.sent-2411-313: Recently, OpenAI o1 and DeepSeek R1 have demonstrated the scalability of unstructured CoT by guiding models to autonomously learn reasoning patterns through Reinforcement Learning.
elements.sent-2411-314: However, reasoning models still suffer from issues of overthinking and excessive computational consumption.
elements.sent-2411-315: Other studies have guided multimodal models to generate structured CoT by providing manually designed templates~\cite{llavacot,llamavo1 .}
elements.sent-2411-316: Although these models incorporate visual semantic information into the reasoning process, their fixed steps constrain the diversity of reasoning actions, limiting their generalization ability on complex problems.

elements.sent-2411-317: Lightman et al. \cite{lightman2023let constructed a process supervision dataset composed of extensive human annotations, which has been widely used for mathematical reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-318: Recent advancements have focused on automating the data acquisition process, allowing models to generate their own CoTs.
elements.sent-2411-319: Techniques like Quiet-STaR~\cite{zelikman2024quiet have demonstrated how self-generated reasoning can enhance model performance without requiring manual labels.}
elements.sent-2411-320: Moreover, some methods based on Monte Carlo estimation have automated the process of data collection, but they also introduce additional computational cost~\cite{Math-shepherd,OmegaPRM .}
elements.sent-2411-321: In multimodal domain, MAVIS~\cite{zhang2024mavis , a dataset consisting of 834k visual math problems annotated with short CoT, has been proposed.}

elements.sent-2411-323: We present the details of AtomThink for promoting MLLM reasoning with self-structured CoT in this section.
elements.sent-2411-324: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:framework , AtomThink consists of four key components, including a self-structured reasoning mechanism (Sec.~\ref{seq:scot ), a data engine (Sec.}}
elements.sent-2411-325: To enable MLLMs to adaptively generate diverse reasoning paths in response to various problems mirroring human cognition, we have proposed an inference method based on Self-structured Chain-of-Thought (SCoT).
elements.sent-2411-326: In contrast to structured methodologies, our approach does not constrain the model to a fixed template of thought or a predefined sequence of reasoning steps.
elements.sent-2411-327: Instead, it empowers the model to autonomously seek optimal reasoning behaviors at inference time.
elements.sent-2411-329: We commence by defining the minimal predictive action with semantic consistency as an Atomic Step, which may constitute a single sentence or a combination thereof.
elements.sent-2411-330: Utilizing atomic steps as fundamental building blocks, we propose a multi-round prediction method to iteratively self-generate thought chains with dynamic structures.
elements.sent-2411-331: During the reasoning process, we prompt the model to predict only one minimal atomic step at a time, thereby focusing on the quality of each atomic step.
elements.sent-2411-332: Subsequently, the current prediction is appended to the historical reasoning steps and provided as contextual input for the next prediction cycle.
elements.sent-2411-333: Our reasoning template with SCoT is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:prompts_atomthink at Appendix~\ref{appendix: prompts .}}
elements.sent-2411-334: Due to the limitations of the model's instruction-following capability, we often observe hallucinations during reasoning, e.g. repetitive atomic steps and duplicated sentences within steps.
elements.sent-2411-335: It can cause the reasoning process to fall into self-referential loops and stagnation.
elements.sent-2411-336: Therefore, we use the following methods for anomaly detection and thought restart:.
elements.sent-2411-337: Rule-based Filter: We employ template matching and Jaccard similarity to assess quantify intra- and inter-step semantic repetition, thereby mitigating looping phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-338: Given the observed potential for partial token mutations and imperfect matches in intra-step repetitions, we set the allowable repetition threshold at below 45\% while the inter-step repetition rate should remain under 98\%.
elements.sent-2411-339: Additionally, we define $max\_step\_length$ and $max\_length$ parameter to control the maximum length of a single atomic step and max response.
elements.sent-2411-340: Temperature Accumulation: Upon detection of an anomaly, we will perform a single-step inference anew to replace the erroneous atomic step.
elements.sent-2411-341: To enhance diversity of outcomes, we incrementally increase the temperature with each error to simulate diverse thinking strategy of human cognition.
elements.sent-2411-342: Policy Search with Process Reward Model.
elements.sent-2411-343: Given that the model spontaneously segments atomic steps during reasoning, a natural consideration is the introduction of a Process Reward Model (PRM) to further expand the search space for predictive actions.
elements.sent-2411-344: Unlike traditional token-based or sentence-based search strategies, we sample candidates using atomic steps as the fundamental unit.
elements.sent-2411-345: As there are many search strategies to generate candidate actions, we categorize the existing strategies into path-wise searching and step-wise searching.
elements.sent-2411-346: In path-wise search, we build upon prior work \cite{wang2024openr,snell2024scaling by parallel sampling multiple paths and aggregating scores to find optimal solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-347: We investigate the following two methods:.
elements.sent-2411-348: It assumes that consensus across different paths is more likely to lead to correct answer.
elements.sent-2411-349: Best-of-N: Given a generative MLLM, best-of-N sampling method generates n candidate rollouts simultaneously and selects solution with the highest score.
elements.sent-2411-350: The evaluation of candidate reasoning processes is determined by PRM, which employs three aggregation methods to map the dense scores to overall value of entire path: 1) The worst action: Compare the worst action among all candidate rollouts.
elements.sent-2411-351: The explainability and consistency of intermediate reasoning are emphasized here as important as outcome.
elements.sent-2411-352: Step-wise search strategies start with an initial path and incrementally expand the sampling space for each atomic action.
elements.sent-2411-353: Greedy Algorithm: It focuses on making locally optimal choice at each step of the reasoning process.
elements.sent-2411-354: It selects the best immediate action (step) based on current state, without considering future consequences.
elements.sent-2411-355: Table~\ref{tab:search provides comparative experiment of different policy search methods.}
elements.sent-2411-356: In our main experiment, we employed a step-wise beam search to extend the inference time.

elements.sent-2411-383: Similar to human problem-solving processes, a SCoT may involve multiple reasoning abilities.
elements.sent-2411-384: However, traditional CoT methods do not focus on the ability to follow individual reasoning step or provide fine-grained analyses of the underlying abilities.
elements.sent-2411-385: To address this gap, we have developed an atomic capability evaluation strategy, offering a new analytical perspective for reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-386: To this end, we first construct a canonical set of capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-387: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:pie_outline , we collect the behavior distribution of GPT-4o on AMATH dataset and use Kimi-1.5 to perform clustering, yielding clusters that each of them represents a certain ability utilized by high-level intelligent models in solving mathematical problems.}
elements.sent-2411-388: We consider each cluster as a set and let $Set(a)$ denote the cluster of an ability $a$.
elements.sent-2411-389: We initially posit that models with superior atomic reasoning capabilities are more adept at leveraging recent contextual steps to further excavate answers.
elements.sent-2411-390: Hence, we can quantify a certain reasoning ability of a model based on its average probability of reaching a correct answer with its rollouts sampled from the corresponding ability set.
elements.sent-2411-391: Specifically, assume a question has $n$ historical reasoning steps $S=\{s_i|i =1,...,n)\}$.
elements.sent-2411-392: We define the step utilization rate $u(S)$ as the probability of reaching an answer by continuing to reason based on $S$ averaging on $M$ sampled rollouts: \begin{equation}u(S) = \frac{\sum^M_{m=1}\llbracket {r}_{m}  is correct\rrbracket}{M}, \label{rate}\end{equation} where $r_m$ is the $m$-th rollout.
elements.sent-2411-393: Subsequently, we calculate the utilization rates of different historical steps and map the corresponding $S$ back to the set of atomic capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-394: We compute the average utilization rate for each category in the ability set to represent the model's atomic reasoning capability, which can be represented as follows, \begin{equation}Score(a) = \frac{1}{{|Set(a)|}}\sum\limits_{{S_k} \in Set(a)} {u({S_k})} . \label{ability_score}\end{equation} In our experiments, we selected 160 samples from an out-of-distribution mathematical dataset (R1V-Stratos \cite{yu25r1vision ), to construct a test set for atomic capability evaluation.}
